draft.introduction: The Instruction of Ani (c. 1200 BCE) is a New Kingdom wisdom text notable for its domestic focus and for including a remarkable dialogue in which the son talks back to the father, questioning whether wisdom can truly be taught.

translation.text: The son replies: “Do not make your word so heavy. I am a young man. I will do what I can. But the heart of a son is not yet wise enough to hear the sayings of his father. A goose that is raised in the field cannot be made to go into the garden by command alone.”
translation.original_text: pā šrj ḥr wšb: m jr md.t=k dns, jnk šrj nḫs. jr.j mn.t=j. nn pā jb n sā r sḏm md.wt n jt(j)=f

translator_notes.note: The son’s objection—that wisdom cannot simply be imposed—is unique in ancient Near Eastern instruction literature. The goose metaphor (a wild creature cannot be domesticated by words alone) introduces a remarkably modern skepticism about education through precept.
